messages.author: Magdalena Kuźmicz Private
messages.body:
Hello Basia and Doron,
Following up on our recent chats, I’m connecting you both regarding Doron’s family tree project.
Basia, meet my friend Doron. Doron, meet Basia, my cousin’s wife. I’ve shared the file below for reference.
Please feel free to reach out to each other with any questions or ideas.
Best of luck with the research! 😉🤞

messages.author: Doron Rapaport
messages.body:
Thank you very much Magda, honored to be called your friend :)
Nice to make an acquaintance Basia :)
Our thoughts are to see if we can get something here quite fast to be able to give it as present for my father's 80th birthday on August.
The idea is to find birth certificate, marriage certificates, any recorded history before and during the war with interst regarding whole family but emphasis on my grandparents - David and Leah Rapaport and my uncle Shimon Rapaport.

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
Hello Magda and Doron,
1. Trochę o mnie. Genealogią zajmuję się amatorsko, ale już od kilkunastu lat. Zaczęłam od mojej rodziny, która pochodzi z Pomorza. Potem zajęłam się Wielkopolską w której mieszkam. I jakoś tak wyszło, że już prowadzę poszukiwania prawie w całej Polsce. Dostępność akt jest różna. W Wielkopolsce i na Pomorzu można w zakresie wyznania katolickiego dotrzeć do XVIII nawet badając rodziny chłopskie, ale nie wszędzie się to udaje.
2. Zapoznałam się z danymi które przesłałeś i zrobiłam drzewo w moim programie.
Proszę sprawdź czy się nie pomyliłam.
3. Wstępnie rozpoznałam jakie dane można już znaleźć on-line w bazach genealogicznych i archiwach. Dygitalizacja materiałów archiwalnych w Polsce i indeksacja akt idzie dosyć szybko, a nowe materiały pojawiają codziennie.
4. Materiały dotyczące osób wyznania mojżeszowego są raczej słabo dostępne. Ale na terenie zaboru pruskiego, czyli właśnie w Wielkopolsce i na Pomorzu od 1874 wprowadzono cywilne akty w zakresie małżeństw, urodzin i zgonów. I tam jest szansa na znalezienie materiałów. Dla Wielkopolski znajdują się w Archiwum Państwowym w Poznaniu i jego oddziałach. Część jest społecznie zindeksowana, część też jest dostępna w formie skanów.
5. Generalnie to mam jedno podstawowe pytanie: czy jesteście pewni, że chodzi o MOSINĘ (miasteczko) pod Poznaniem?
W Polsce w obecnych granicach są jeszcze wieś Mosina w lubuskim i wieś Mosina w zachodniopomorskim.
Nie wiem ile wiesz o historii Polski, ale mieliśmy tu zabory i okupacje, zmieniały się granice. Prawie za każdym razem zmieniały się nazwy miejscowości. Jeśli Twoja rodzina opuściła Polskę po wojnie lub w jej trakcie to mogła zapamiętać jakąś starszą wersję nazwy.
Mosina pod Poznaniem w czasie okupacji miała nazwę „Moschin”
Dlatego potrzebne są chociaż fragmenty wspomnień. Na przykład takie, że jeździli też do synagogi w Poznaniu, że ktoś się uczył w Poznaniu.
W internecie jest bardzo dobrze opracowana księga meldunkowa Poznania z lat 1870-1931 nazwisko Rapaport występuje tylko 22 razy a Rapoport 15. To bardzo mało, bo w Wielkopolsce było stosunkowo niewielu Żydów, chociaż w 1907 powstała w Poznaniu potężna synagoga (stoi do dziś, w fatalnym stanie, budynek chociaż przetrwał wojnę, ma pecha, gmina żydowska sprzedała ją w 2019 roku).
6. Podobnie jest z nazwiskami. W wyniku transkrypcji na alfabet łaciński z cyrylicy lub hebrajskiego pojawiają się różne wersje nazwiska dla tej samej osoby, co po latach grzebania w materiałach archiwalnych mnie nie dziwi. Dla waszej rodziny przyjmuję więc, że szukamy Rapaporta, Rapoporta, Rappaporta… i innych pokrewnych, bo urzędnik mógł być trochę głuchy lub nietrzeźwy lub osoba kopiująca akty robiła błędy, albo zwyczajnie bazgrał.
Dla przykładu: w mojej rodzinie pojawiają takie wersje nazwiska: Kosznik (współczesna), Kośnik, Koschnik, Koschnick (zniemczone).
Nazwisko rodowe Leah Vitsner jest bardzo nietypowe, podejrzewam, że jest zniekształcone.
7. Dlaczego wątpię w podane miejsce urodzenia twojego Dziadka.
Powtarzająca się w twoim drzewie Mosina sugeruje, że Rapaportowie byli rodziną zasiedziałą w jednym miejscu. Natomiast w Mosinie pod Poznaniem: „Pierwsza wzmianka o ludności żydowskiej zamieszkującej Mosinę pochodzi z 1793 r. W spisie przeprowadzonym przez władze pruskie, wylicza się 20 obywateli wyznania mojżeszowego na 419 mieszkańców. Spis ludności z 1800 r., podaje, iż w mieście zamieszkuje 25 Żydów – 6 krawców, 3 gorzelników i jednego producenta wina. Od tego czasu następuje dynamiczny wzrost liczby mieszkańców wyznania mojżeszowego. W roku 1840 – 166 (blisko 16,5% ogółu mieszkańców), a w roku 1871 – 206 obywateli. To najwięcej w całej historii miasta. Systematyczny odpływ ludności żydowskiej z Mosiny rozpoczął się po roku 1871 - po Zjednoczeniu Niemiec i proklamacji II Rzeszy. Zjawisko to wiązało się właśnie z emigracją żydowskich mieszkańców na teren Niemiec. Masowy charakter emigracja ta przybrała po zakończeniu I wojny światowej, w konsekwencji czego w 1921 r. w Mosinie mieszkało zaledwie 22 Żydów. W 1922 r. liczba ta zmalała do 6-ciu. W latach 30. ubiegłego stulecia, w Mosinie nie było już ani jednego mieszkańca wyznania mojżeszowego. Synagoga opustoszała, a władze miasta planowały adaptować ją na swoją siedzibę. Według niektórych przekazów, w synagodze miała mieścić się w latach przedwojennych szkoła ewangelicka.”
Akurat akta urodzenia z Mosiny z roku 1911 są dostępne on-line i nie ma tam David Rapaport. Istnieje nikła szansa, że może zgłoszono go urzędu  na początku 1912, ale to już musiałabym sprawdzić osobiście w archiwum.
Natomiast żona David – Leah, wg waszych informacji, urodziła się w Stanisławowie. Jest to popularna nazwa w Polsce, ale zakładam wstępnie, że chodzi o dzisiejszy Iwano-Frankiwsk obecnie na Ukrainie, bo ludność żydowska mieszkała głównie w miastach.
To rodzi pytanie jak mieliby się poznać David z podpoznańskiej Mosiny i Leah ze Stanisławowa. Znów trzeba sięgnąć do strzępów wspomnień.
To nie jest zupełnie nieprawdopodobne, ponieważ przed wojną wielu Żydów przyjeżdżało do Puszczykowa - miejscowości pomiędzy Poznaniem i Mosiną, które było popularnym letniskiem. O tym, że było ich wielu świadczy fakt, że w 1938 polska, prawicowa, nacjonalistyczna partia polityczna zrobiła niechlubną akcję pod nazwą „Plaże nad Wartą bez Żydów” i oznaczyła plażę jako strefę wolną od Żydów.
Taka dygresja. Temu, że w Polsce w niektórych kręgach przed wojną był antysemityzm nie ma co zaprzeczać. Mam w rodzinie przykład człowieka, który był w tej partii, był bardzo antyniemiecki i równocześnie był antysemitą. Niemcy w 1939 rozstrzelali jego oraz jego syna.
8. Ponieważ, jak może wiesz od Magdy, interesuje się też II wojną światową to korzystam z baz odnoszących się do tego okresu, szczególnie jak coś mi się nie zgadza.
Znalazłam tam David Rapaport (ur. 25.12.1911!) z Polski, który wojnę przeżył, tak jak twój Dziadek (wnioskuję z daty i miejsca urodzenia twojego Ojca jubilata) Miejsce urodzenia jest nieczytelne, albo raczej wymaga wysiłku przy jego odczytaniu, natomiast jest informacja, że przed wojną mieszkał we Lwowie (teraz Ukraina). Są też inne informacje, ale druk jest po angielsku, wypisywany ręcznie po francusku, da się go odczytać przy pewnym staraniu.
Magdo: jak mój Wojtek nie da rady to rzucimy zagadkę Kasi.
Zgadza się więc data urodzenia i to, że przeżył i pojechał do Izraela. Dokument został sporządzony w „B.” 9.04.1946.
Ale imiona Ojca i Matki są zupełnie inne. Więc mamy zagadkę.
Czy Twój Dziadek znał włoski i niemiecki?
9. Przy poszukiwaniu korzeni liczy się każda, często z pozoru nieistotna informacja, bo może wykluczyć lub potwierdzić jakąś hipotezę.
Już widzę, że łatwo nie będzie. Nie wiem na ile macie ciągłość pamięci w rodzinie, pokolenia wojenne, a nawet te pierwsze powojenne niewiele mówiły. Otwierały się zazwyczaj pod koniec życia, ale historia rodzin tkwi w nas, przekazana podświadomie.
W przypadku Shimon Rapaport ur 1936 będzie problem z dokumentami związany z RODO, bo nie minęło jeszcze 100 lat. Nie ma szans na zlezienie on-line jego aktów stanu cywilnego. Gdybyśmy odkryli gdzie rzeczywiście są (jeśli są w Polsce) to trzeba udowodnić pokrewieństwo i złożyć wniosek.
Magdo: wytłumacz o co chodzi z RODO, bo ja wymiękam w tym irytującym temacie.
Nie mam Żydów w rodzinie. Szukałam, sami pobożni katolicy ;)  Mojego brata, gdy jeździł po świecie często pytano czy nie jest Żydem :) Pasowało, że ma ciemne włosy, oczy i zajmuje się logiką. Poszukiwanie Twoich przodków to dla mnie nowa przygoda, ale jeśli nie będę potrafiła do czegoś dotrzeć to się do tego przyznam i może znajdę kogoś innego. Czasu nie jest dużo (gdyby to jednak była Wielkopolska to damy radę) ale na wszelki wypadek pomyślcie o innym, awaryjnym, prezencie. Choć taki jaki planujecie byłby fajny!
1. A little about me. I've been doing genealogy as an amateur for over a decade now. I started with my family, which comes from Pomerania. Then I focused on Greater Poland, where I live. And somehow, I've been conducting research almost everywhere in Poland. The availability of records varies. In Greater Poland and Pomerania, it's possible to trace Catholic ancestry back to the 18th century, even by researching peasant families, but this isn't always possible.
2. I've reviewed the data you sent and created a tree in my program.
Please check if I made any mistakes.
3. I've made a preliminary assessment of what data can already be found online in genealogical databases and archives. The digitization of archival materials in Poland and the indexing of records are progressing quite rapidly, and new materials are appearing daily.
4. Materials concerning people of the Jewish faith are rather scarce. But in the Prussian Partition, specifically in Greater Poland and Pomerania, civil records regarding marriages, births, and deaths were introduced in 1874. And there's a chance to find materials there. For Greater Poland, they're in the State Archives in Poznań and its branches. Some are publicly indexed, and some are also available as scans.
5. Generally, I have one basic question: are you sure you're referring to MOSINA (a small town) near Poznań?
Within Poland's current borders, there's also the village of Mosina in the Lubusz Voivodeship and the village of Mosina in the West Pomeranian Voivodeship.
I don't know how much you know about Polish history, but we've had partitions and occupations here, and borders have changed. Town names have changed almost every time. If your family left Poland after or during the war, they might remember an older version of the name.
Mosina, near Poznań, was called "Moschin" during the occupation.
That's why we need at least fragments of your memories. For example, that they also went to the synagogue in Poznań, that someone studied in Poznań.
There's a very well-documented Poznań registration book online from 1870-1931. The surname Rapaport appears only 22 times, while Rapoport appears 15. This is very few, as there were relatively few Jews in Greater Poland, despite the fact that a massive synagogue was built in Poznań in 1907 (it still stands today, in terrible condition. Although the building survived the war, it's unfortunate; the Jewish community sold it in 2019).
6. It's similar with surnames. Transcriptions into the Latin alphabet from Cyrillic or Hebrew lead to different versions of the same surname, which, after years of digging through archival materials, doesn't surprise me. For your family, I assume we're looking for Rapaport, Rapoport, Rappaport... and other related names, as the official might have been slightly deaf or intoxicated, or the person copying the deeds made mistakes or simply scribbled.
For example, in my family, the following versions of the surname appear: Kosznik (modern), Kośnik, Koschnik, Koschnick (Germanized).
Leah Vitsner's maiden name is very unusual; I suspect it's a corruption.
7. Why do I doubt your grandfather's birthplace?
The recurring Mosina in your tree suggests that the Rapaports were a long-established family. In Mosina near Poznań: "The first mention of the Jewish population living in Mosina dates back to 1793. The census conducted by the Prussian authorities listed 20 citizens of the Jewish faith out of 419 inhabitants. The census of 1800 shows that the town had 25 Jews – 6 tailors, 3 distillers and one wine producer. Since then, there has been a dynamic growth in the number of Jewish inhabitants. In 1840 – 166 (nearly 16.5% of the total population), and in 1871 – 206 citizens. This is the highest number in the entire history of the town. The systematic outflow of the Jewish population from Mosina began after 1871 – after the Unification of Germany and the proclamation of the Second Reich. This phenomenon was related to the emigration of Jewish inhabitants to Germany. This emigration became mass after the end of World War I. World War II, as a result of which, in 1921, only 22 Jews lived in Mosina. In 1922, this number decreased to six. By the 1930s, there were no longer any Jewish residents in Mosina. The synagogue was deserted, and the city authorities planned to convert it into their headquarters. According to some sources, the synagogue housed an Evangelical school in the prewar years.
Birth records from Mosina from 1911 are available online, and David Rapaport is not listed there. There is a slim chance that he was registered with the authorities in early 1912, but I would have to check that in person in the archives.
However, David's wife, Leah, according to your information, was born in Stanisławów. It is a common name in Poland, but I am tentatively assuming that you are referring to present-day Ivano-Frankivsk, now in Ukraine, as the Jewish population lived primarily in cities.
This raises the question of how David from Mosina, near Poznań, and Leah from Stanisławów could have met. Again, we have to delve into fragments of memories.
This isn't entirely improbable, Because before the war, many Jews came to Puszczykowo – a town between Poznań and Mosina, which was a popular summer resort. The fact that there were many of them is evidenced by the fact that in 1938, a Polish right-wing nationalist political party launched the infamous campaign "Beaches on the Warta River Without Jews" and designated the beach as a Jew-free zone.
Just a side note. There's no denying that anti-Semitism existed in some circles in Poland before the war. I have a family example of a man who was a member of this party, was very anti-German, and was also an anti-Semite. In 1939, the Germans shot him and his son.
8. Since, as you may know from Magda, I'm also interested in World War II, I use databases relating to that period, especially when something doesn't add up.
I found David Rapaport (born December 25, 1911!) from Poland, who survived the war, just like your grandfather (I'm judging from the date and place of birth of your father, the celebrant). The place of birth is illegible, or rather, requires effort to decipher, but there is information that he lived in Lviv (now Ukraine) before the war. There is other information, but the print is in English, handwritten in French, and can be read with some effort.
Magdo: If my Wojtek can't figure it out, we'll throw the puzzle to Kasia.
So the date of birth and the fact that he survived and went to Israel are correct. The document was prepared in "B." on April 9, 1946.
But the father's and mother's names are completely different. So we have a puzzle.
Did your grandfather know Italian and German?
9. When searching for roots, every piece of information, often seemingly insignificant, counts, as it can rule out or confirm a hypothesis. I can already see it won't be easy. I don't know how much continuity of memory you have in your family; the wartime generations, and even the first post-war generations, didn't say much. They usually opened up at the end of life, but family history is embedded in us, passed down subconsciously.
In the case of Shimon Rapaport, born 1936, there will be a problem with documents related to RODO, because it hasn't been 100 years yet. There's no chance of finding his civil status records online. If we discover where they actually are (if they're in Poland), we'll have to prove kinship and submit an application.
Magda: Explain what this RODO :(  thing is about.
I don't have Jewish family members. I've been looking, all devout Catholics ;) My brother, when he traveled around the world, was often asked if he was Jewish :) It suited me that he has dark hair and eyes and is a logician. Searching for your ancestors is a new adventure for me, but if I can't find anything, I'll admit it and maybe I'll find someone else. There's not much time (if it were Greater Poland, we'd manage), but just in case, consider another, emergency gift. Although the one you're planning would be great!

messages.author: Doron Rapaport
messages.body:
Dear Basia,
As I am reading this, first of all wow! How deep and smart you are ! Magda was right (as always).
My father is the only person alive now from his family, and he was born actually in Brussels after the war at 1946.
My grandmother wrote a book about the war but some of the information is mixed and therefore I do not know. The pdf I gave is from Rapaport research institute in Jerusalem and I can not verify anywhere.
At her book, my grandmother Leah Lusha Rapaport (Vitsner before) was actually born in Bolochow or maybe Bolechow Hebrew בולוכוב. She did not write where my grandfather David Rapaport was born. She mentioned that she moved from Bolochow to Levov but does not mention that she met my grandfather there.
She does write that my grandfather moved to Mosina to mange a wood sawmill factory which his family owned at Mosina. I also know for sure that my grandfather was a wood expert because this was afterwards his speciality while he was working at customs authority management in Israel.
My grandmother writes they married in a small town named Mosina and seems that my uncle Shimon was born there because my grandmother is telling a story about going with him to a river in Mosina. Per the story they lived in Mosina until 1939.

messages.author: Doron Rapaport
messages.body:
In another place she writes that she lived with my granddaughter's parents in Przemyśl for a while after Mosina.
Then they moved to Nadvorna per her story then came back to Levov..

messages.author: Doron Rapaport
messages.body: This is actually what we are trying to learn:)

messages.author: Doron Rapaport
messages.body: The only thing I know about my family tree is that my grandfather was David (Memek) Rapaport my grandmother was Leah (Lusha) Rapaport (of Vitsner family) and my uncle was Shimon Rapaport.  They are all no longer with us.

messages.author: Doron Rapaport
messages.body:
My grandfather knew German to some extent and was really smart person.
I trust you can find more since you already did so much!
Thanks very much!

messages.author: Doron Rapaport
messages.body:
Good evening Basia,
What do you think, any chance to get something more?
Thanks in advanced

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
Właśnie badam czy coś jeszcze uda się znaleźć. Chciałam do ciebie napisać
1. Jeszcze czekam na opinię mojego specjalisty od francuskiego ;) Wojtka.  Dokument który znalazłam jest kluczowy. Jest na nim odręczny podpis Davida Rapaporta, który powinieneś zweryfikować. Może znajdziesz jakiś stary dokument po dziadku gadzie jest jego odręczny podpis. Jeśli będzie pasować to możemy iść dalej.
2. Moim zdaniem powinniśmy przestać szukać w Wielkopolsce. To co napisałeś ostatnio pozwoliło mi na taką ocenę. Sprawdziłam klo był właścicielem tartaków w Mosinie pod Poznaniem. Były dwa, jeden należał do Władysława Litke, drugi Władysława Urbaniaka. Imię Władysław jest tradycyjnie polskie.
3. Historia przenosi się do wschodniej Galicji (województwo lwowskie i stanisławowskie) i nie tylko.
4. Ze wspomnień Babci wynika, że trzeba iść za drewnem.
Udało mi się ustalić, że prawidłowa przedwojenna nazwa miejsca jej urodzenia to BOLECHÓW, miasteczko na południe od Stryja.
Od 1883 była tam „Niższa Cesarsko-Królewska Szkoła Leśna”, która od 1922 działała jako Państwowa szkoła dla leśniczych.
Myślę, że pisownia nazwiska Babci powinna brzmieć Fitzner lub Ficner, bo w wersji Vitsner w polskich bazach nie ma nikogo.
5. We wspomnieniach Babci nazwy miejscowości są poprzekręcane. Lewów to po prostu Lwów, a Nadworna to Nadwórna (czytaj Nadwurna) w dawnym województwie stanisławowskim położona u podnóża Karpat na południe od Stanisławowa.
6. Największy problem jest nadal z domniemaną Mosiną. Sprawdziłam w„Index of Placenames of the Republic of Poland with corresponding govermental agencies and offices, including communication facilities” 1933 , że była tylko jedna Mosina - ta w Wielkopolsce.
Zastanawiam się właśnie jakie inne miasteczko mogło mieć podobną nazwę i mam już jakiś pomysł.
7. Znalazłam w województwie lwowskim tartak należący do Rapaporta i myślę jak to można zweryfikować
8. Z tych danych, które mamy da się na pewno jeszcze coś wyciągnąć, ale najważniejsza jest weryfikacja dokumentu.
Poczekaj jeszcze trochę jak to się rozwinie, bo może wspólnymi siłami coś osiągniemy. Czy w miejscu pracy Dziadka w Izraelu są jakieś jego dokumenty? Może trzeba przeszukać archiwa agencji celnej, w Europie takie rzeczy trzeba trzymać długo.
I'm currently investigating whether I can find anything else. I wanted to write to you.
1. I'm still waiting for my French expert's opinion ;) Wojtek. The document I found is crucial. It has David Rapaport's handwritten signature on it, which you should verify. Maybe you'll find an old document from your grandfather, and his handwritten signature is there. If it matches, we can move on.
2. I think we should stop looking in Greater Poland. What you wrote recently allowed me to make that assessment. I checked, and Kło owned sawmills in Mosina near Poznań. There were two, one belonging to Władysław Litke, the other to Władysław Urbaniak. The name Władysław is traditionally Polish.
3. The story moves to eastern Galicia (Lviv and Stanisławów Voivodeships) and beyond.
4. Grandma's memories suggest that we have to go after the wood. I managed to determine that the correct pre-war name of her birthplace was BOLECHÓW, a town south of Stryj.
From 1883, there was a "Lower Imperial-Royal Forestry School" there, which from 1922 operated as a state school for foresters.
I think the spelling of Grandma's surname should be Fitzner or Ficner, as the Vitsner version doesn't appear in Polish databases.
5. In Grandma's memoirs, the place names are misspelled. Lewów is simply Lwów, and Nadworna is Nadwórna (read: Nadwurna) in the former Stanisławów Voivodeship, located at the foot of the Carpathians south of Stanisławów.
6. The biggest problem remains with the supposed Mosina. I checked the "Index of Placenames of the Republic of Poland with corresponding government agencies and offices, including communication facilities" from 1933, and found that there was only one Mosina – the one in Greater Poland.
I'm wondering what other town might have had a similar name, and I already have an idea.
7. I found a sawmill belonging to Rapaport in the Lviv Voivodeship and I'm wondering how I can verify it.
8. I can certainly glean something from the data we have, but the most important thing is to verify the document.
Wait a bit longer to see how this develops, because maybe together we can achieve something. Are there any of his documents at Grandpa's place of work in Israel? Maybe we should search the customs agency archives; in Europe, such things have to be kept for a long time.

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
No i coś się znalazło.
W Archiwum Głównym Akt Dawnych w Warszawie przechowują zespół ksiąg metrykalnych gmin wyznania mojżeszowego z terenów tzw. zabużańskich.
On-line dostępny jest: „gmina wyznaniowa Bolechów powiat Dolina, księga metrykalna urodzeń z lat 1907-1918”.
Wklejam wyjaśnienia do zawartości zespołu archiwalnego:
Omawiany zespół tworzą księgi urodzonych, zaślubionych i zmarłych oraz zachowane w niewielkim stopniu indeksy osobowe. Indeksy te wszywano do ksiąg lub sporządzano oddzielnie. Księgi urodzonych zawierają wszelkie dane dotyczące narodzonego dziecka: imiona, daty oraz miejsca urodzenia i obrzezania lub nadania imienia, płeć, status, imiona i nazwiska rodziców, miejsce zamieszkania oraz nr domu, imiona i nazwiska oraz miejsce zamieszkania rodziców matki dziecka (sporadycznie także rodziców ojca), a także informacje o zatrudnieniu i miejscu zamieszkania kumów lub świadków, obrzezujących, akuszerki oraz uwagi. Zwraca uwagę znaczna liczba dzieci „nieślubnych” zarejestrowanych w księgach. Dziecko uznawano za nieślubne i jako takie wpisywano do ksiąg, gdy jego rodzice zawarli jedyne tradycyjne małżeństwo rabinackie i nie zarejestrowali tego związku we właściwym urzędzie. Wówczas rubryka dotycząca ojca pozostawała niewypełniona. W księgach zaślubionych wpisywano imiona i nazwiska, miejsce urodzenia, zatrudnienie, miejsce zamieszkania, nr domu, wiek i stan narzeczonych oraz imiona, zatrudnienie i miejsce zamieszkania ich rodziców, datę i miejsce ślubu, miejsce zamieszkania rabina asystującego przy zaślubinach, a także miejsce zamieszkania świadków wraz z informacją o ich zatrudnieniu oraz uwagi. W księgach zmarłych zamieszczano szczegółowe dane odnoszące się do zmarłego (imię, nazwisko, stan, płeć, wiek, zatrudnienie, miejsce zamieszkania, informacje o rodzicach i współmałżonkach), daty i miejsca śmierci i pogrzebu, nr karty oględzin potwierdzonej przez zwierzchność i nazwisko oglądającego zwłoki, adnotację o chorobie i rodzaju śmierci oraz uwagi. Jak wspomniano wcześniej, w księgach występują także różnego rodzaju notatki będące z reguły urzędowymi zaświadczeniami dotyczącymi osoby, której akt urodzenia, ślubu czy zgonu figuruje w aktach. Szczególnie często pojawiają się informacje o późniejszym urzędowym zawarciu związku małżeńskiego przez rodziców i uznaniu ich dziecka za pochodzące z legalnego związku.
Pytanie: jaka była różnica wieku między David a Leah? W danych które mi przekazałeś nie ma daty urodzenia Leah.
Ten zbiór ma prawie 200 stron kart ręcznego pisma, mogę przeglądać wszystko, ale może coś pamiętasz, n.p. w jakim była wieku gdy umarła.
Przyszło mi jeszcze do głowy, że przecież imiona rodziców powinny być w akcie zgonu Davida, umarł w Izraelu, pewnie możesz do tego dotrzeć zupełnie oficjalnie, jesteś wnukiem. Tak to działa w Polsce.
Poniżej przykładowy, przypadkowy skan ze zbioru.
*And something has been found* .
The Central Archives of Historical Records in Warsaw maintains a collection of vital records of Jewish communities from the so-called Bug River region.
The following is available online: "Bolechów religious community, Dolina district, birth record book from 1907-1918."
I am pasting explanations of the contents of the archival collection:
The collection in question consists of birth, marriage, and death records, as well as sparsely preserved personal indexes. These indexes were sewn into the records or prepared separately. The birth records contain all data concerning the born child: names, dates and places of birth and circumcision or naming, gender, status, parents' names and surnames, place of residence and house number, names and surnames and place of residence of the child's mother's parents (occasionally also the father's parents), as well as information on the employment and place of residence of the kin or witnesses, circumcisers, midwives, and notes. The significant number of "illegitimate" children registered in the registers is noteworthy. A child was considered illegitimate and entered as such when its parents entered into a single, traditional rabbinical marriage and failed to register their union with the appropriate office. In such cases, the father's name was left blank. Marriage registers included the first and last names, place of birth, employment, place of residence, house number, age, and marital status of the engaged couple, the first and last names, employment, and marital status of their parents, the date and place of the wedding, the place of residence of the rabbi assisting at the wedding, and the place of residence of the witnesses, along with their employment information and comments. The registers of deaths included detailed information about the deceased (first and last name, marital status, gender, age, employment, place of residence, information about parents and spouses), the date and place of death and burial, the number of the postmortem examination card confirmed by the authority and the name of the person who viewed the body, a note about the illness and manner of death, and comments. As mentioned earlier, the books also contain various notes, usually official certificates concerning the person whose birth, marriage, or death certificate is included in the records. Information about the subsequent official marriage of parents and the recognition of their child as the result of a legal union is particularly common.
As a result of the First Partition of Poland, Austria occupied the southern part of the Kraków and Sandomierz Voivodeships, as well as the Ruthenian Voivodeship. These lands, known as the Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria, fell under the strong influence of Josephine policies. The Patent of Tolerance of 1781 did not cover the Mosaic religion. At the same time, in accordance with the principles of Josephine's reform, the Imperial Patent of March 15, 1782, established Catholic parish priests as civil registrars for non-Christian denominations as well. The general regulations regarding entries in the parish registers of 1784 stipulated that official registers containing birth, marriage, and death certificates would be maintained solely by the Catholic parish priest. Israelite parish registers, compiled by municipal officials (rabbis), were henceforth exclusively private. Fundamental changes to the legal norms concerning the Jewish faith were introduced by the imperial decree of May 7, 1789, which established 141 religious communities in Galicia, uniting the Jewish population there, and established general principles for the organization of the Jewish community. These were confirmed and expanded 100 years later by the imperial decree of March 21, 1890. The 1789 decree permitted the practice of the Jewish religion without hindrance and granted synagogues the same rights as Catholic churches. The maintenance of the Jewish community records was entrusted to a rabbi. However, it was not until the decree of July 10, 1868, that these records were finally declared legally binding and the control of Jewish records by Catholic priests was abolished. The principle of maintaining records by a rabbi remained in effect until 1875, when it was abolished by a rescript of the Imperial-Royal Ministry of Internal Affairs dated March 15, 1875, concerning the maintenance of records of Israelites in Galicia, supplemented by detailed instructions. This function was entrusted to separate officials approved by state authorities. The record books from the earlier period were closed and handed over to new officials for official use. Monitoring of record keeping was exercised by the relevant administrative authorities (starosty). Communities were required to submit one of two copies of the records to the starosty in whose jurisdiction they were located after completing the entries for the given calendar year. On April 2, 1891, by a decree of the Imperial-Royal Ministry of Religious Affairs and Education, the final administrative and territorial division of Galician Jewish communities was implemented. From then on, 252 Jewish religious communities operated in the Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria, including the Grand Duchy of Kraków, with approximately 160 in the territories conventionally known as the "Beyond the Bug River." After Poland regained independence in 1918, the Jewish population found itself among the religious minorities living within the borders of the Second Polish Republic. The Jewish communities became part of the Jewish Religious Union, an organization established on November 1, 1916, in the former Russian partition. A decree of the Chief of State of February 7, 1919, legalized the existence of this union, simultaneously adapting its structure to the requirements of Polish statehood. A decree of the President of the Republic of Poland of October 14, 1927, extended the validity of this decree to the Kraków, Lviv, Stanisławów, and Tarnopol voivodeships, uniting the Jewish inhabitants of these areas with the Jewish population of the former Russian partition into a single public religious association. At the same time, the March Constitution of 1931 guaranteed the Jewish communities associated with the association the right to govern themselves by their own laws, recognized by the state. Israelite registry books acquired the force of civil status records, but state authorities retained the right to supervise the activities of rabbis, obliging Jewish religious communities to send duplicate birth, marriage, and death certificates to the district offices (decree of the President of the Republic of Poland of March 16, 1928). This organization remained in place until the outbreak of World War II.
Question: What was the age difference between David and Leah? The data you provided me doesn't include Leah's date of birth.
This collection has almost 200 pages of handwritten pages. I can look through everything, but maybe you remember something, like how old she was when she died.
It also occurred to me that David's parents' names should be on his death certificate; he died in Israel. You can probably access that officially; you're his grandson. That's how it works in Poland.
Below is an example, random scan from the collection.

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
Jestem Ci winna wyjaśnienie. Napisałeś, że twój dziadek zarządzał rodzinnym tartakiem. Jest taka ogromna księga adresowa dla handlu, przemysłu, rzemiosł i rolnictwa z 1930 roku. Można ją przeglądać on-line, ale nie działa na niej wyszukiwarka, bo ma 3000 stron. Już kiedyś z niej korzystałam i umiem się w niej poruszać, choć jest to czasochłonne. Sprawdziłam ile było tartaków w województwie lwowskim i kto był właścicielem. Znalazłam ten jeden, należący do Rapaport S. i Liptan M.  A jak jest połączony to jeszcze nie wiem, weryfikuję po prostu waszą wiedzę. W tej chwili można powiedzieć tylko, że jakiś Rapoport miał tartak i w tym zakresie opowieść się zgadza. Wierzbiany są położone na północny zachód od Lwowa, blisko dzisiejszej polskiej granicy między Niemirów a Jawor.
W rodzinnych opowieściach często się zdarzają wątki, że jakaś nieruchomość należy do rodziny a później okazuje się, że chodzi o dzierżawę.
Jeszcze jedna uwaga. Trudność z nazwami miejscowości może polegać też na tym, że w języku polskim nazwy własne się odmienia (deklinacja). Pisząc do ciebie próbuję tego nie robić, ale grubym napisała poprawnie to brzmiałoby to tak: „między Niemirowem a Jaworem”.  Sądzę, że Twoja Babcia pisząc „Lewowa” właśnie zastosowała deklinację, choć  zrobiła to niepoprawnie bo powinno być „Lwowa”. Chociaż Żydzi zamieszkujący Lwów, którzy mówili w jidysz używali nazwy Lemberg.
Zastanawiam się nad tym szukając tej „Mosiny”
I owe you an explanation. You wrote that your grandfather managed the family sawmill. There's a huge directory for trade, industry, crafts, and agriculture from 1930. You can browse it online, but the search engine doesn't work because it's 3,000 pages long. I've used it before and I know how to navigate it, though it's time-consuming. I checked how many sawmills there were in the Lviv Voivodeship and who owned them. I found this one, belonging to Rapaport S. and Liptan M. I don't know yet how it's connected; I'm simply verifying your knowledge. Right now, all I can say is that a certain Rapoport had a sawmill, and in that respect, the story is correct. Wierzbiany is located northwest of Lviv, close to today's Polish border between Niemirów and Jawor.
Family stories often involve a property belonging to a family, only to later discover it was a lease.
One more note. Another difficulty with place names can be that proper names in Polish are declined (declension). I'm trying to avoid this when writing to you, but if she had written it correctly in bold, it would have read: "between Niemirowem and Jaworem." I think your grandmother used declension when writing "Lewowa," though she did it incorrectly because it should have been "Lwowa." Although the Jews living in Lviv, who spoke Yiddish, used the name „Lemberg”.
I'm wondering about this while looking for this "Mosina"

Wklejam dokument. Zrobiliśmy konsylium. Uważam, że jest bardzo prawdopodobne, że dotyczy Twojego Dziadka. Ale musimy zmienić miejsce poszukiwania Twoich Pradziadków.
Ale po kolei:
1. To jest kartoteka osób przebywających poza ojczyzną, która była prowadzona po wojnie. Chodzi o tzw. DIPIS czyli Displaced Persons. „B” – oznacza albo Belgia albo Bruksela. Pieczątka na drugiej stronie dotyczy pewnie przyjęcia dokumentu  1969 do Belgijskiego Komisariatu Repatriacji przy Ministerstwie Zdrowia Publicznego i Rodziny
2. Dane  odczytujemy w ten sposób:
Rapaport David, żonaty, Polak
urodzony 25.12.1911 w Cieszynie (Tesin) w Polsce, wyznania mojżeszowego
imię ojca Bernard
imię matki  Regina (prawdopodobnie)
nazwisko panieńskie Grunfeld
pożądana destynacja Palestyna lub U.S.A.
miejsce przebywania 1. stycznia 1938 Lwów Polska
zawód inżynier leśnictwa
deklarowana znajomość języka w kolejności płynności:
a. polski
b. włoski, niemiecki
c. czeski, rosyjski
nie był jeńcem
zarejestrowany 9.04.1946 w B.
uwagi:  pozbawiony obywatelstwa w 1942 (prawdopobnie)
3. Nie pokazywałam dokumentu, bo te dane są zaskakujące i musiałam się upewnić czy dobrze myślę. Miejsce urodzenia jest zapisane w jego czeskiej wersji. Miasto po polsku to Cieszyn, po czesku Tesin, a po niemiecku Teschen. W 1911 nie było ani Polski ani Czech tylko Austro-Węgry. Obecnie miasto jest podzielone granicą na Olzie na Cieszyn i Czesky Tesin. To jest w Zachodniej Galicji, obecnie w województwie śląskim.
Tekst zamieszczony w uwagach jest zaplamiony, ale wydaje się ważny. Po naradzie postawiliśmy na używane w Belgii „deforce”.
Magdo, może jeszcze Kasia sprawdzić.
Jeśli Doron potwierdzisz podpis, to mamy ciekawą opowieść. Zaczynam się domyślać co się mogło wydarzyć, ale te poszukiwania mają zwroty akcji!

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
I'm attaching the document. We've conducted a consultation. I believe it's very likely that it concerns your grandfather. But we need to change the location where we're looking for your great-grandparents.
But first things first:
1. This is a file of people residing outside their homeland, which was kept after the war. It's the so-called „DIPIS”, or Displaced Persons. "B" stands for either Belgium or Brussels. The stamp on the back probably refers to the 1969 document being accepted by the Belgian Repatriation Commission at the Ministry of Public Health and Family.
2. We read the data as follows:
Rapaport David, married, Polish
born December 25, 1911, in Cieszyn (Tesin), Poland, of the Jewish faith
father's name Bernard
mother's name Regina (probably)
maiden name Grunfeld
desired destination Palestine or the U.S.A.
Location January 1, 1938, Lviv, Poland
Profession: Forestry Engineer
Declared language proficiency in order of fluency:
a. Polish
b. Italian, German
c. Czech, Russian
He was not a POW
Registered: April 9, 1946, in B.
Notes: He was stripped of his citizenship in 1942 (probably).
3. I didn't show the document because the information is surprising and I had to make sure I was correct. His place of birth is recorded in its Czech version. The town is Cieszyn in Polish, Tesin in Czech, and Teschen in German. In 1911, there was neither Poland nor the Czech Republic, only Austria-Hungary. Currently, the town is divided by the border on the Olza River into Cieszyn and Český Tesin. This is in Western Galicia, now in the Silesian Voivodeship.
The text in the notes is stained, but it seems important. After deliberation, we settled on the "deforce" used in Belgium.
Magda, Kasia can check.
If Doron confirms the signature, we have an interesting story. I'm starting to guess what might have happened, but this search has its twists and turns!
Magdalena Kuźmicz Private added +33 6 82 81 13 25

messages.body:
Good evening Basia
Past days we have been trying to locate any documentation from my grandfather without success.
I know my grandmother had older sister Tzipira , a younger brother named Moses and a small sister named Pnina.
At the end of the war grandparents together with Shimon they arrived from Lyvov to Katowice and From there by bus to Brussels. My father Bernard Dov Rapaport was born in Brussels.
My grandfather escaped from Nadworns geto where he had a work permit to work in a sawmill to Lyvov. He escaped in a rail way wagon between wooden logs inside a hidden niche he carved in the wooden logs.
They met at Rapaport street in lyvov. My grandmother was living at legionov str 24 in the ghetto under Hormek fake documents.
For a period, they lived apart from each other. David was working at the railway in road construction.
Lea was a manger of art affects store under fake identity.
Per the story, this is around 1941.

messages.author: Basia
messages.body: My daughter made dinner, and I had to step away from the computer. I want to tell you a story. My father, after his 75th birthday, began writing his memoirs, focusing on the occupation. I read them, but the children were young and life was rushing by... After a while, I realized something was missing. About a year before his death, I asked him, "Why aren't there any Jews in your story?" That's when he started crying and telling the story. The family had to leave their apartment in Tczew, Pomerania, and flee. He was 13 years old. They settled in Sierpc, on the border with Masovia. They took over a tiny house by the Sierpienica River. On the other side was the ghetto. He couldn't talk about it without sobbing, even at the end of his life. He was shaking. He honestly told me that after the ghetto was liquidated, Poles rushed to search for "Jewish gold." And, yes, he felt he had been dealt a "better hand." Although he also had to use all his wits to survive. I learned very late that his older cousin was on the first transport to Auschwitz (from the prison in Tarnów). But... the family managed to get him out. His father, my grandmother's brother, was sent there later, but he survived. He was a goldsmith, watchmaker, and engraver. If he had been Jewish, they would have included him in Operation Bernhard. My father-in-law's stepfather was arrested in September 1939 as a hostage in place of his brother (who owned a sawmill). He remained in the camp until the end of the war. They experimented on him. My grandmother's brother, the first in the family to graduate from university, had no chance. He was executed as part of the operation to eliminate the Polish intelligentsia. Survival in that war depended not only on your nationality but also on your location. One day, we were returning from Munich and stopped in Berchtesgaden and drove up the Kehlstein. We ate something from this "gift to Hitler from the people for his 50th birthday." Then I went to the toilet and realized (as an engineer) that my excrement was flowing into the same pipe as Hitler and Eva Braun. The laughter of history. I represented the genes of all those in my family who perished because of him. And I'll tell you about my mother's family later, because it's very difficult...

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
1. Proszę, spróbujcie przeczytać poniższy artykuł (nie wiem jaka będzie jakość tłumaczenia a artykuł jest pisany ciężkim językiem naukowym). Artykuł naświetla realia w Przemyślu w czasie gdy była tam Lusia. Na czerwono zaznaczyłam fragment dotyczący wydawania paszportów.
To jest opracowanie naukowe IPN. Proponowałam Wam zwrócenie się tam w sprawie Rapaportów. Jak widzicie temat ich interesuje. Tylko nie jesteśmy w stanie przewidzieć co mogą znaleźć …
Sprawa Żydów w NKWD jest delikatna. Pogrzebałam w internecie i pamięci (coś mi się przypomniało, że była jakaś awantura dyplomatyczna). Znalazłam artykuł dotyczący sporu pomiędzy Izraelem a Litwą, który został odnotowany w naszej prasie (w „Rzeczpospolitej”, a to jest gazeta dbająca o rzetelność). Pewnie łatwiej będzie Wam odnaleźć informacje po hebrajsku.
„Yad Vashem: nie wolno ścigać Żydów z NKWD
Litewski minister został wyrzucony z konferencji o Szoah. To reakcja na śledztwa przeciw ocalałym z Holokaustu” (2011).
Oczywiście to są skrajne przypadki.
Yad Vashem: nie wolno ścigać Żydów z NKWD - rp.pl
Nie wiem czy ten link otworzy się w Izraelu.
Problem z NKWD, które zostało zastąpione przez znane nam wszystkim KGB, polega na tym, że to rzadko była normalna praca. Nie można było tak łatwo powiedzieć „ja dziękuję, już nie chcę dla was pracować”. Może Lusia, wyjeżdżając w 1945 uciekała także przed NKWD?
Deklaracja kierunku wyjazdu, którą złożył Dawid – Palestyna lub U.S.A może na to wskazywać. W dokumentach, które mamy z okresu gdy przebywali w Polsce (1945-1946) jest tylko Lusia, nie ma Dawida, za to w Brukseli jest Dawid a nie ma Lusi. Ale to tylko hipoteza, próba zrozumienia tego co się mogło wydarzyć.
„TOMASZ BEREZA, IPN Office in Rzeszów
HISTORICAL COMMENTARY NKVD REPRESSIONS AGAINST RESIDENTS OF THE BORDER ZONE
1939–1941
The turn of 1989 brought an opportunity to eliminate "blank spots" in Poland's recent history. Historians focused their attention on, among other things, Polish-Soviet relations between 1939 and 1989. Particular attention was paid to research on the repressions against Polish citizens who found themselves under Soviet occupation in 1939–1941.
The Katyn affair, mass deportations deep into the USSR, and the activities of the NKVD have been the subject of numerous publications. However, there are research issues that have been addressed only marginally or are omitted due to a lack of access to sources. Issues related to repression against border residents, resettlement and destruction of property, and the tightened police regime have received limited research.
	The first steps to secure the new USSR border were taken during the delimitation process. The impetus for these actions came from the directives of Lev Mekhlis, head of the Red Army's Main Political Directorate (APD), who, in an order dated October 2, 1939, warned the political officers of the Belarusian and Ukrainian fronts against complacency, urged vigilance in the face of capitalist encirclement, and called for the construction of border fortifications.
The military's actions demonstrated more distrust of its new citizens than of its new neighbors. The border strip, fortified by farmers as part of the "shrunkards," and the machine gun nests on the bridgeheads were intended to hinder escape to the German occupation zone rather than secure the border against aggression. Work on the comprehensive fortification of the Western Ukrainian border was initiated by an order from the Chief of Staff of the Ukrainian Front, Komdiv Nikolai Vatutin, dated October 15, 1939. Units of "płotniki" (carpenters) were deployed to all sections of the border, building barbed wire and wooden-and-earth shelters, and erecting "videts" (observation towers).
Liberation
The above decisions were made even before Western Ukraine was officially incorporated into the Ukrainian SSR. On October 6, the Ukrainian Front command announced the rules and dates for the elections to the People's Assembly of Western Ukraine, which was to "decide" the fate of the lands "liberated" by the Red Army. The election announcement was signed by Semyon Timoshenko and Nikita Khrushchev. After a short election campaign, the vote took place on October 22nd, which, according to official data, was successful and validated the public's mandate of trust in the People's Assembly's decisions – voter turnout was reportedly 92.8 percent, and support for the candidates reached 90.9 percent of the total votes cast. In reality, the elections were a complete farce: the People's Assembly of Western Ukraine met in Lviv for its first and only session from October 26–28. Among the political and economic decisions made at that time was a request to the Supreme Soviet (SC) of the USSR to admit Western Ukraine into the Ukrainian SSR. Meeting on November 1–2, 1939, the Supreme Council of the USSR naturally acceded to the request of "representatives of the working masses of cities and villages." Two weeks later, on November 15, the Supreme Council of Ukraine accepted Western Ukraine into the republic. This was a significant development, as it heralded the introduction of Soviet administrative structures and legal norms. As a result of two administrative reforms (December 4, 1939, January 17, 1940), voivodeships and counties were abolished, replaced by oblasts and raions. The territories of the former Yaroslavl and Lubaczów counties were incorporated into Lviv Oblast.
Passportization
On November 29, the Soviet Supreme Council issued a decree on the acquisition of Soviet citizenship by residents of the western regions of the Ukrainian and Byelorussian SSRs. The population of the annexed territories was covered by the Soviet passport instruction of December 27, 1932, according to which passports were available to persons over sixteen years of age, permanently residing in cities, workers' settlements, and within a 7.5 km zone from the border, and working in state farms, transport, and new construction sites. The implementing regulations for this so-called passportization were issued by the USSR NKVD only on February 5, 1940; in the Lviv Oblast, it began on March 1 and was completed in May 1940. The passportization operation provided an excellent pretext for the NKVD's meticulous vetting of citizens. By June 5, 1940, nearly 300,000 people had been issued passports in the Western Ukrainian border region. Refusal to accept a passport (or for it to be issued by the NKVD) meant deportation or arrest. Other legal regulations in force in the USSR were also applied to the new border. On January 29, 1940, the Central Committee of the All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks) approved the introduction of three border zones: seven and a half kilometers, eight hundred meters, and four meters. This decision led to the resolution of the Council of People's Commissars (RKL) of the USSR on the Protection of the State Border in the Western Oblasts of the Ukrainian SSR of March 2, 1940, according to which the forced expulsion of all residents of the eight-hundred-meter border zone began.
Deportation
The exceptions were towns, including Przemyśl, Uhnów, and Sokal. The resolution of the RKL merely confirmed the existing status quo, as the deportation of entire towns from the border strip took place in February 1940. The deportation and resettlement operation was finally completed in the winter of 1940/1941. As a result, residents of 13 villages and hamlets in the Lubaczów and Jarosław counties were deported.
In total, over 100,000 people were resettled from the western regions of the Ukrainian SSR based on the aforementioned resolution.
Those resettled to Bessarabia were the most "lucky," as they received information about the deportation 24 hours in advance and, at their destination, settled on farms abandoned by Romanian Germans. […]
Border Protection
All legal regulations mentioned above can be classified as "passive" border protection, as opposed to "active" protection, i.e., the direct actions of designated services. Initially, border protection was the responsibility of the AC (Army of the Armed Forces), which were soon replaced by operational groups and then by units of the Border Protection Troops of the NKVD of the Kyiv Military District. The commander of the NKVD Border Protection Troops in the Kyiv District was Komdiv V. Osokin, and later Major General V. Khomenko. Due to the border shift, the district headquarters was moved from Kyiv to Lviv. It is worth noting that the territorial changes did not affect the security of the existing Polish-Soviet border, where border zone status continued and border troops operated.
The full staff of the guard post was 50-80 people, including a political officer and non-commissioned officers, who usually constituted 10 percent of the crew. The network of guard posts, staffed by several dozen men, was dense; there were 15–25 NKVD WOP soldiers per kilometer of the border. This number also included members of the so-called auxiliary brigades, recruited from local communists, whose ranks in Western Ukraine and Western Belarus reached 25,000 (as of January 1941). Anti-personnel barriers and alarm devices were erected along the deserted border strip, making illegal border crossings difficult. The parish priest in Oleszyce (Lubaczów County; in 1940–1941 the Lubaczów region), Father Józef Mroczkowski, described the border zone as follows: "The area 800 meters from the border wire was already a penal zone. It was marked with separate barbed wire, natural obstacles (cut down trees), bells and signal plates, as well as traps and rocket-propelled grenades. It was forbidden to even approach these barbed wire.
Guards patrolled the fields during field work. Looking towards the border was forbidden."
Escapes
Both the introduced legal restrictions and the physical security of the border failed to deter attempts to cross it illegally. The reasons for this practice were twofold: on the one hand, shortages of goods and the resulting contraband; on the other, the escapes of those who felt "at risk" and had to flee for political ("class") reasons. Smuggling from the German to the Soviet side included saccharin, lighter fluid, headache powders, clothing, and even bread; initially, Polish złoty, especially silver coins, as well as kerosene and salt, were smuggled in the other direction. Those seeking to reach the German occupation zone included those who, due to their origin or position held before 1939, faced arrest or deportation, officials and military personnel along with their families, the intelligentsia, and refugees from western Poland who had fallen under Soviet occupation in September 1939. Jews were the main refugees fleeing in the opposite direction. Attempts to illegally cross the border often ended in arrest or death. It's worth emphasizing that Soviet border guards were much more rigorous in their duties than their "colleagues" from the Grenzschutz. Furthermore, an extensive network of informants worked for the NKVD. The activities of a certain Spindel in Oleszyce have become almost legendary. Spindel, competing with other NKVD agents, denounced dozens of people, for which he received the Order of Lenin.
In combating illegal border crossings, provocation was often used. An example of such actions is the incident that took place in the winter of 1940 in Mołodycz (Jarosław County, Sieniawa Oblast in 1940–1941). A man arrived in this town from the direction of Lviv, posing as a guide across the "green border." He quickly gathered a group of volunteers who wanted to escape to the German-occupied area. Shortly thereafter, the NKVD arrived in Mołodych and arrested nine people, using a list prepared by the provocateur. All those apprehended were likely murdered in the Lviv "Brygidki" prisons.
Victims
The number of victims across the entire border must have been significant, given that within the operational area of ​​just four guard posts of the 92nd Border Guard Unit of the NKVD, in the Szówsko-Sieniawa section, the author counted eight people killed while crossing the border and 12 arrested (and subsequently disappeared) for simply attempting to cross it. The above figures are minimal and have been verified, and the fact that repression was used in these cases is beyond doubt.
For the entire western border of the USSR from 1939 to 1941, the number of victims reached at least several thousand. These figures require verification, as official Soviet historiography classified those affected as spies (even breeding pigeons near the border was considered espionage). Without ruling out
such a possibility, especially in the period preceding the outbreak of the German-Soviet war (June 22, 1941), it seems justified to begin research as soon as possible into the toll of victims of the "final Soviet-German border of interests." This demand is dictated by the circumstances: the generation that remembers the period of Soviet occupation is passing away, and is often the only source
of information about these tragic events.”

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
1. Please try reading the article below (I don't know what the quality of the translation will be, and it's written in a rather academic style). The article highlights the realities in Przemyśl during Lusia's time there. I've highlighted the section about the issuance of passports in red.
This is a research paper by the Institute of National Remembrance (IPN). I suggested you contact them regarding the Rapaports. As you can see, they're interested in the topic. We just can't predict what they might find...
The issue of Jews in the NKVD is a delicate one. I've searched the internet and my memory (I remembered something about a diplomatic row). I found an article about the dispute between Israel and Lithuania, which was reported in our press (in "Rzeczpospolita," a newspaper that cares about accuracy). You'll probably find it easier to find information in Hebrew. "Yad Vashem: Jews must not be prosecuted by the NKVD"
A Lithuanian minister was expelled from a conference on the Shoah. This is a reaction to the investigations into Holocaust survivors (2011).
Of course, these are extreme cases.
Yad Vashem: Jews must not be prosecuted by the NKVD - rp.pl
I don't know if this link will open in Israel.
The problem with the NKVD, which was replaced by the KGB we all know, is that it was rarely a normal job. You couldn't just say, "No thanks, I don't want to work for you anymore." Perhaps Lusia, when she left in 1945, was also fleeing the NKVD?
David's declaration of departure – Palestine or the U.S. – may indicate this. In the documents we have from the period they were in Poland (1945-1946), only Lusia is listed, not Dawid. However, in Brussels, there is Dawid, but no Lusia. But this is just a hypothesis, an attempt to understand what could have happened.

The bankruptcy primarily concerned Eisig Chaim Griffel, or rather his sons, and occurred in 1930, involving a company engaged in oil extraction and processing. As you can see, the case dragged on for a long time. However, they partially secured their personal assets. Instead, they developed companies related to the timber industry. Their lawsuit was widely reported and commented on in the press. They relied on family connections in their business, and when they went bankrupt, they also took the Lamm family, the family of Rebecca's sister's husband, with them.
Nationwide reports of the trial contain details describing the family. I need to elaborate on this.

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
Why am I so certain about Muszyna?
I finally came across the compiled minutes of the Muszyna Commune Council (28.10.1933). It includes the following excerpt:
"Item 2 concerned offers for the purchase of timber. Therefore, the chairman announced that the Council had received two offers for the sale of 1,000 m3 of fir timber with a starting price of PLN 8 per m3. The first bidder was the Zygmunt Griffel Company from Muszyna, offering PLN 8.40 per m3. It was also stated that if the offer was accepted, the aforementioned company would pay the entire amount due for 1,000 m3 of timber in advance. The second offer was submitted by Chastel Horowitz, a native of Tylicz, offering PLN 8.30 per m3. It is easy to guess which offer the Commune Council accepted. A letter was officially sent to Zygmunt Griffel from the "forest sawmill in Muszyna" approving the acceptance of the aforementioned offer.

messages.author: Basia
messages.body:
Dziękuję za udostępnienie książki o Lusi. Muszę ją przeczytać kilka razy. Tekst jest rzeczywiście trudny. Ale ze znajomością polskiego kontekstu jest łatwiej.
Na razie nie dzwoniłam do archiwum, muszę to przemyśleć w kontekście dat podanych w książce i sekwencji wydarzeń.
Na stronie 86 Lusia wspomina o powojennej podróży na Słowację do bogatych krewnych o nazwisku GRYPEL.  Chodzi oczywiście o rodzinę GRIFFEL, z którą rzeczywiście Dawid był spokrewniony i związany. Przymiotnik „bogactwo” mógł dotyczyć tylko okresu przedwojennego. Na Słowacji rządziła partia komunistyczna, wtedy nikt nie był bogaty. Później można poszukać tej rodziny. Być może rzeczywiście zmienili nazwisko na Grypel.
Jeszcze wrócę do Muszyny.
Interesy rodziny Griffel były prowadzone na dużą skalę i nie zawsze pod własnym nazwiskiem. Mnożyli firmy, sprzedawali je, likwidowali.
W tekście, z który chciałam porównać opis pobytu Lusi w Muszyna  w 1939 roku autor (Rafał Żebrowski) podaje, że właścicielem tartaku była  żydowska firma „Segal-Żupnik-Wilf”. To mnie początkowo zmyliło. Jednak w „Przegląd drzewny” z 1938 roku w artykule „Eksport kompletów do Palestyny” wśród firm z małopolski wymieniono – *„Zygmunt Griffel Lwów (Segal, Żupnik, i Wilf* )”. Więc wszystko pasuje.
Artykuł ma tytuł: „ *_Żydzi w Muszynie wobec Holokaustu_* ”  To rzetelny artykuł opisujący historię lokalną. Niestety tartak został zamieniony w obóz.
 Jest jeszcze drugi artykuł: Stanisław Stojek „ *_Miasto i gmina Muszyna w czasie drugiej wojny światowej_* ”
Autor pisze, że 3-5 września, do Muszyny weszły wojska słowackie i przygraniczna ludność cywilna, rabując opuszczone domy wczasowe, pensjonaty, a nawet domy prywatne. Po południu 6 września wchodzą Niemcy. Gestapo przybyło do Muszyny na początku grudnia 1939 roku.
W odniesieniu do Żydów pisze:
„ _Niemcy po wkroczeniu do Muszyny natychmiast zajęli się Żydami mieszkającymi w tym mieście. Już w początkowej fazie swych rządów wprowadzili dla tej grupy przymus pracy fizycznej, a następnie przymus noszenia białej opaski na ramionach z sześcioramienną gwiazdą Dawida. Nakazali zgolić brody i wąsy, a modlitewniki spalić. Zabronili handlu, a towar w sklepach zarekwirowali_ .
_Tuż przed świętami Bożego Narodzenia zarządzili konfiskatę wszystkich posiadanych przez Żydów książek i polecili złożenie ich w jednym punkcie w pomieszczeniach obok naszej Gminy. Żydzi przynieśli książki i zapełnili nimi cały pokój. Następnie książki spalono_ .”
Według tego autora ok. 200 Żydów opuściło Muszyna uciekając z wojskiem polskim na wschód, a około 600 pozostało.
Wywnioskowałam z książki, że Lusia, Dawid i Szymon nie uciekli w tej pierwszej fali.
Autor pisze też: „ _Bogatsi Żydzi na własną rękę emigrowali na Słowację i Węgry_ .” Ostatni Żydzi opuścili Muszynę w końcu 1940 roku.
Rzeczywiście był więc moment na zastanowienie się i zapoznanie z sytuacją. Charakterystyczne w książce jest to, że Lusia nie wymienia tych dat i pisze tak jakby okres krótko przed wojną i początek wojny zlewały jej się w jedno.
Myślę, że Dawid polecił jej wyjazd do Przemyśla na przełomie listopada i grudnia 1939 roku.
Inny autor Andrzej Moszczak w artykule „ *_Wspomnienia z lat okupacji_* ” pisze: „ _Taki rozkwit trwał do roku 1939, kiedy to w połowie sierpnia – w obliczu zbliżającej się wojny – nastąpił gwałtowny wyjazd kuracjuszy i turystów z Krynicy oraz Muszyny. [...]_
Hotel, który prowadziła Lusia nazywał się Willa „Helin” i mieścił się przy ulicy Ogrodowej.
W artykule „ _*Narodziny muszyńskiego uzdrowiska*_ ” autorzy Stanisław Heilman, Jerzy Krzyś piszą: „ _Wkrótce też zaczęto wznosić obiekty murowane, ze sporym – jak na owe czasy – komfortem („Zosia”, „Aleksandrówka”, „Helin” itp.)_ ”
Gestapo zajęło willę „Helin”. W 1992 odsłonięto tam tablicę ku czci męczonych w tej katowni. Rapaport nie mieli więc do czego wracać w Muszynie.
Jest jeszcze taki polski wątek. W tym budynku przesłuchiwano złapanych polskich kurierów przeprowadzających ucienkinierów na słowacką stronę,  w tym słynną polską narciarkę Helena Marusarzówna.
Muszę teraz zająć się Przemyśl.
Thank you for sharing the book about Lusia. I need to read it several times. The text is indeed difficult. But knowing the Polish context makes it easier.
I haven't called the archives yet. I need to think about it in the context of the dates given in the book and the sequence of events.
On page 86, Lusia mentions a post-war trip to Slovakia to visit wealthy relatives named GRYPEL. This, of course, refers to the GRIFFEL family, to whom Dawid was indeed related and connected. The adjective "wealthy" could only refer to the pre-war period. Slovakia was ruled by the Communist Party, and no one was wealthy back then. You can look for this family later. Perhaps they did indeed change their surname to Grypel.
I'll return to Muszyna.
The Griffel family's businesses were conducted on a large scale and not always under their own name. They multiplied companies, sold them, and liquidated them. In the text I wanted to compare with the description of Lusia's stay in Muszyna in 1939, the author (Rafał Żebrowski) states that the sawmill was owned by the Jewish company "Segal-Żupnik-Wilf." This initially confused me. However, in the 1938 issue of "Przegląd Drzewny," in the article "Export of sets to Palestine," among the companies from Lesser Poland, "Zygmunt Griffel Lwów (Segal, Żupnik, and Wilf)" was listed. So everything fits.
The article is titled: "Jews in Muszyna and the Holocaust." It's a thorough account of local history. Unfortunately, the sawmill was turned into a concentration camp.
There's also a second article: Stanisław Stojek, "The City and Commune of Muszyna during World War II."
The author writes that on September 3-5, Slovak troops and border civilians entered Muszyna, looting abandoned holiday homes, guesthouses, and even private homes. On the afternoon of September 6, the Germans entered. The Gestapo arrived in Muszyna in early December 1939. Regarding the Jews, he writes:
"After entering Muszyna, the Germans immediately took action against the Jews living in the city. Already in the initial phase of their rule, they introduced compulsory physical labor for this group, and later forced them to wear a white armband with a six-pointed Star of David. They ordered them to shave their beards and mustaches, and to burn their prayer books. They banned trade and confiscated goods in stores.
Just before Christmas, they ordered the confiscation of all books owned by Jews and ordered them to be stored in one place in the rooms next to our Community. The Jews brought the books and filled the entire room with them. Then the books were burned."
According to this author, approximately 200 Jews left Muszyna, fleeing eastward with the Polish army, and approximately 600 remained.
I concluded from the book that Lusia, Dawid, and Szymon did not escape in this first wave. The author also writes: "The wealthier Jews emigrated on their own to Slovakia and Hungary." The last Jews left Muszyna at the end of 1940.
There was indeed time to reflect and familiarize yourself with the situation. It's characteristic of the book that Lusia doesn't mention these dates, writing as if the period shortly before the war and the beginning of the war were merging into one.
I believe Dawid recommended her to Przemyśl at the turn of November and December 1939.
Another author, Andrzej Moszczak, writes in his article "Memories from the Occupation Years": "This boom lasted until 1939, when in mid-August – in the face of the approaching war – there was a sudden departure of spa guests and tourists from Krynica and Muszyna. [...]
The hotel Lusia ran was called the Willa "Helin" and was located on Ogrodowa Street.
In the article "The Birth of Muszyna's Spa," authors Stanisław Heilman and Jerzy Krzyś write: "Soon brick buildings began to be built, with considerable comfort for the time ("Zosia," "Aleksandrówka," "Helin," etc.)."
The Gestapo did indeed seize the "Helin" villa. In 1992, a plaque was unveiled there to commemorate those tortured in this torture chamber. The Rapaports, therefore, had nothing to return to in Muszyna.
There is also a Polish element. Captured Polish couriers who were escorting refugees to the Slovak side, including the famous Polish skier Helena Marusarzówna, were interrogated in this building.
Now I must turn to Przemyśl.

To jest podanie o paszport złożone 29.02.1924.
U góry imię i nazwisko ojca:      Rapaport Mojżesz Saul
lewa kolumna:
- nazwisko i imię petenta : Rapaport Berisch,
- imię ojca Mojżesz Saul,
- adres: Tarnów Folwarczna 9
środkowa kolumna:
- rysopis: wzrost średni, twarz pociągła, włosy czarne, nos usta proporcjonalne, oczy czarne,
- dzieci do lat 14 włącznie Mendel 12 lat, Lotti 8 lat
prawa kolumna:
- nr dokumentu osobistego wydanego przez Starostwo L 19376/22,
- miejsce faktycznego zamieszkania ostatnio w ciągu 6 miesięcy Stanisławów Kościuszki 4,
- obecny dokładny adres Berisch Rapaport, Stanisławów Kościuszki 4,
- miejsce zapisania do ksiąg ludności stałej organizacji stanowej ewent. przynależność gminna Dąbrowa,
- ze strony policji przeszkód do wydania paszportu zagranicznego do Wiednia nie ma żadnych.
I kto z Was jest podobny do tego Pana ze zdjęcia? :)
Namierzyłam też akt urodzenia, znowu miałam szczęście, bo nie wszystko z Tarnowa jest on-line
W akcie są następujące informacje:
Urodził się Tarnowie 6 sierpnia 1884 roku.
Imię podane w akcie to „Benzion”.
Adres: „Tarnów miasto nr 24”
Ojciec Mojżesz Rapaport nauczyciel prywatny w Tarnowie
Matka Rywka Schiff z Tarnowa córka Michała Schiffa kramarza i Cywie Schiff z Tarnowa.
I na szczęście Tarnów jest w Polsce a nie na Ukrainie.
Ulica Folwarczna w Tarnowie to teraz ulica Waryńskiego.
A ulica Kościuszki w Stanisławów przed wojną była w samym centrum.
Jak to się stało, że z Przemyśla powędrowałam w badaniach do Stanisławowa, a potem do Tarnowa to opiszę Wam później. U mnie jest 4 rano i ptaki zaczęły śpiewać, zasiedziałam się przy tych poszukiwaniach ale było warto.
I did a great job of analysis. I eliminated other possibilities and finally got this! I was also a little lucky.
This is a passport application submitted on February 29, 1924.
At the top, father's name: Rapaport Mojżesz Saul
Left column:
- applicant's name: Rapaport Berisch
- father's name: Mojżesz Saul
- address: Tarnów Folwarczna 9
Middle column:
- description: average height, elongated face, black hair, nose and mouth proportionate, black eyes
- children up to 14 years of age: Mendel 12, Lotti 8
Right column:
- personal document number issued by the District Office: L 19376/22
- place of actual residence last 6 months: Stanisławów Kościuszki 4
- current address: Berisch Rapaport, Stanisławów Kościuszki 4
- place of registration in the population register of the permanent organization of the state, or Dąbrowa municipality,
- there are no police obstacles to issuing a foreign passport to Vienna.
And who of you resembles the man in the photo? :)
I also located his birth certificate; again, I was lucky, as not everything from Tarnów is online. The certificate contains the following information:
He was born in Tarnów on August 6, 1884.
The name given in the certificate is "Benzion."
Address: "Tarnów city no. 24"
Father: Mojżesz Rapaport, a private teacher in Tarnów
Mother: Rywka Schiff from Tarnów, daughter of Michał Schiff, a merchant, and Cywie Schiff from Tarnów.
And fortunately, Tarnów is in Poland, not Ukraine.
Folwarczna Street in Tarnów is now Waryńskiego Street.
And Kościuszki Street in Stanisławów was in the very center before the war.
I'll describe later how I ended up traveling from Przemyśl to Stanisławów, and then to Tarnów, for my research. It's 4 a.m. here, and the birds are singing. I've been looking for a while, but it was worth it.
